findings.description: Multiple small dark objects (at least 4–5 discrete spots) scattered across the flat/low-slope roof surface. While some are consistent with standard roof penetrations or vent boots, several are irregularly distributed and do not align with a typical vent pattern, indicating loose items or debris on the membrane.
findings.location_description: Central flat roof section, distributed across the full deck within the orange bounding box

findings.description: Roof surface is a bright light gray/white — distinctly different from every surrounding neighbor home, which all show dark gray asphalt shingles. This is consistent with a TPO, EPDM, or coated flat membrane roof, which carries its own maintenance and inspection considerations compared to a pitched shingle roof.
findings.location_description: Entire visible flat roof deck within the orange bounding box

rationale: The subject roof within the orange bounding box is a flat or very low-slope section covered in a light-colored membrane (white/light gray), which stands in clear contrast to all adjacent neighboring homes that have dark gray pitched shingle roofs. The surface is largely uniform with no visible tarps, large discoloration patches, structural sag along the roofline, or moss growth. However, several small dark objects are scattered across the membrane deck that do not conform to a standard vent/penetration layout, warranting a closer ground-level look to confirm whether these are equipment, debris, or damage indicators. Notably, construction lumber and materials are visible stacked in the yard immediately adjacent to the home on the east and south sides, suggesting active or recent renovation work that may be relevant to the roof's current state. No missing material, structural deformation, or tarping is visible from this altitude and angle.
